channel.title: Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Accelerating Expansion
item.description:
The accelerating expansion is the observed fact that the expansion of the universe is speeding up rather than slowing down. It was discovered in 1998 and is caused by dark energy.

Before 1998, cosmologists expected the expansion to be decelerating. Gravity attracts, so the mutual gravitational pull of all the matter in the universe should slow the expansion over time, like a ball thrown upward. The only question seemed to be whether the expansion would eventually halt and reverse (a “Big Crunch”) or merely slow toward a coast.

item.title: Big Bang
item.description:
The Big Bang is the event approximately 13.8 billion years ago from which the observable universe expanded from an extremely hot, dense state. It is not an explosion in space. It is the beginning of the expansion of spacetime itself.

The Big Bang model follows from general relativity applied to an expanding universe. If the universe is expanding now, it was smaller and denser in the past. Extrapolate backward and you reach a state of arbitrarily high density and temperature. The model does not describe what happened at the very first instant (the “singularity” at t=0t = 0 is a sign that general relativity breaks down at extreme densities), but it makes specific, testable predictions about what happened shortly after:

item.title: Cosmic Expansion
item.description:
Cosmic expansion is the observed increase in distances between gravitationally unbound objects in the universe, described mathematically by the scale factor a(t)a(t).

Expansion does not mean galaxies are flying apart through space like shrapnel from an explosion. It means space itself is growing. The distances between galaxies (and between galaxy clusters) increase over time because the metric of spacetime is changing. Objects gravitationally bound to each other – stars within a galaxy, planets within a solar system, atoms within a molecule – do not expand, because local gravitational and electromagnetic forces overwhelm the expansion.

item.title: Cosmic Inflation
item.description:
Cosmic inflation is a hypothesized period of exponentially rapid expansion in the first fraction of a second after the Big Bang, proposed to explain several otherwise puzzling features of the observed universe.

Alan Guth proposed inflation in 1981 to solve three problems:

The horizon problem. The cosmic microwave background has nearly the same temperature in every direction, even in regions so far apart that light has not had time to travel between them since the Big Bang. How did they reach the same temperature without being in causal contact? Inflation solves this: those regions were in causal contact before inflation, which then stretched them far apart.

item.title: Friedmann Equations
item.description:
The Friedmann equations are the differential equations governing the expansion of a homogeneous, isotropic universe. They are what you get when you apply general relativity to a universe satisfying the cosmological principle.

Alexander Friedmann derived them in 1922, well before the expansion of the universe was observationally confirmed. Georges Lemaître independently obtained them in 1927. The full spacetime model is called the Friedmann-Lemaître-Robertson-Walker (FLRW) metric.

The first Friedmann equation relates the expansion rate to the energy content:

item.title: Hubble Parameter
item.description:
The Hubble parameter H(t)H(t) is the rate of cosmic expansion at a given time, defined as the ratio of the rate of change of the scale factor to the scale factor itself:

H(t)=a˙(t)a(t)H(t) = \frac{\dot{a}(t)}{a(t)}

Its present-day value, H0H_0, is called the Hubble constant, though it is constant only in the sense that it is evaluated at the current epoch – the Hubble parameter changes over cosmic time.

H0H_0 sets the relationship between a galaxy’s distance and its recession velocity: v=H0dv = H_0 d. This is Hubble’s law. The current best measurements place H0H_0 at approximately 67-73 km/s/Mpc (kilometers per second per megaparsec), meaning that for every megaparsec (about 3.26 million light-years) of distance, the recession velocity increases by 67-73 km/s.

item.title: Nucleosynthesis
item.description:
Nucleosynthesis is the formation of atomic nuclei. Big Bang nucleosynthesis (BBN) refers specifically to the formation of light elements – hydrogen, helium, and trace amounts of lithium and deuterium – in the first few minutes after the Big Bang, when the entire universe was hot and dense enough for nuclear fusion.

At about one second after the Big Bang, the temperature was roughly 10 billion kelvin. Protons and neutrons were in thermal equilibrium. As the universe expanded and cooled, neutrons began converting to protons (neutrons are slightly heavier and decay into protons via the weak nuclear force). By about three minutes, the temperature dropped to about one billion kelvin – still hot enough for fusion. Protons and neutrons combined to form deuterium, then helium-4, with small amounts of helium-3, lithium-7, and other light nuclei.

item.title: Recombination
item.description:
Recombination is the epoch approximately 380,000 years after the Big Bang when the universe cooled to about 3,000 kelvin – cool enough for free electrons to combine with atomic nuclei to form neutral atoms. This made the universe transparent to light for the first time.

The name is somewhat misleading: electrons and nuclei had never been combined before, so “combination” would be more accurate. The name persists for historical reasons.

Before recombination, the universe was a hot plasma of free electrons, protons, and helium nuclei, bathed in intense radiation. Photons scattered constantly off free electrons (Thomson scattering), making the universe opaque – no photon could travel far before being deflected. Matter and radiation were tightly coupled.

item.title: Scale Factor
item.description:
The scale factor a(t)a(t) is a dimensionless function that describes how distances in the universe grow over time. It is the central dynamical variable of cosmology.

If two galaxies are separated by a distance d0d_0 at a reference time t0t_0, their separation at any other time tt is d(t)=a(t)⋅d0/a(t0)d(t) = a(t) \cdot d_0 / a(t_0). By convention, aa is set to 1 at the present epoch. At earlier times a<1a < 1 (the universe was smaller); at later times a>1a > 1 (it will be larger).
